title: Word Break
problem_statement: Given a string s and a dictionary of strings wordDict, return true if s can be segmented into a space-separated sequence of one or more dictionary words.

examples.explanation: The string can be segmented as "leet code".
The string can be segmented as "apple pen apple".

intuition: At each index, determine whether the prefix ending there can be segmented by checking previous valid break points and dictionary words.

approaches.name: Recursive Brute Force
approaches.description: Try every dictionary word at the current prefix and recurse on the remaining suffix.
approaches.why_not: Exponential because it recomputes segmentation decisions for the same suffix many times.

approaches.name: Memoized Recursion (Top-Down DP)
approaches.description: Memoize the result for each starting index to avoid repeated work on the same suffix.
approaches.why_not: This is good, but iterative dp is simpler and avoids recursion.

approaches.name: Bottom-Up Dynamic Programming
approaches.description: Use dp[i] to track whether s[0..i-1] can be segmented, and update it using valid dictionary words ending at i.
approaches.why_not: This is the standard DP approach.

interview_qa.a: It means s[0..i-1] can be segmented into words from wordDict.

interview_qa.q: How can you optimize substring checks?
interview_qa.a: Use a set for O(1) lookup and limit the substring length by the longest dictionary word.
dp[0] = true, representing the empty string.
It prevents reprocessing the same suffix, turning exponential recursion into polynomial time.
